title: Candidemia -- Evaluation
content: Because of the low sensitivity of blood cultures for Candida spp, surrogate markers can also be used to improve diagnostic accuracy. Considering all patient variables, these markers can be considered as supporting evidence when culture results are not yet available in patients at high risk for candidemia. Such markers are Candida mannan antigen, anti-mannan antibodies, and β-d-glucan (BDG). [32] [33] They have the advantage of providing faster results but can have high false-positive rates. The BDG can be used as a negative predictive value. [32] [34] When evaluating a patient suspected of candidemia, 2 sets of blood cultures should be obtained. A non-culture test (eg, BDG), should also be performed to enhance the diagnostic accuracy. [6]

title: Antifungal Agents -- Indications -- Indications
content: Fluconazole indications include the treatment of esophageal, oropharyngeal, peritoneal, urinary tract, and vaginal candidiasis. Fluconazole may also help treat systemic fungal infections, including candidemia, candida pneumonia, and cryptococcal meningitis. Fluconazole serves as a first-line agent in prophylaxis for mycoses in allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant patients. Off-label, fluconazole has a variety of applications, including blastomycosis, empiric antifungal therapy in non-neutropenic ICU patients, Candida prophylaxis (in ICU patients with a high risk of invasive Candida spp and transplant patients), and tinea. [36]

title: Aspergillus Fumigatus (Archived) -- Evaluation
content: In cases of severe invasive aspergillosis an emerging tool has been used especially in intensive care unit patients, beta-(1,3)-d-glucan which is a cell wall component of Aspergillus and useful in cases where the onset of treatment is essential to improve prognosis. This component is also present in other organisms such as Candida , Fusarium , and Pneumocystis . Serum galactomannan is another Aspergillus cell wall component released into the bloodstream during intravascular hyphal growth. However, as it is captured by a neutrophil concentration that may be falsely low which makes its sensitivity lower in non-neutropenic patients. The key test for chronic pulmonary aspergillosis is a positive Aspergillus immunoglobin (IgG) serum test. Only a minority of patients have positive cultures (less than 10%). Elevated galactomannan assay from bronchoalveolar lavage fluid yields higher sensitivity in these cases. Other nonspecific markers are elevated CRP and ESR.

title: Fluconazole -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: Fluconazole is a member of the triazole family and a commonly used antifungal agent. [1] This antifungal agent is FDA-approved to treat vaginal candidiasis, oropharyngeal and esophageal candidiasis, peritonitis, and systemic Candida infections, including candidemia, disseminated candidiasis, pneumonia, and cryptococcal meningitis. Prophylaxis is also known to decrease candidiasis incidence in patients receiving bone marrow transplantation who receive radiation therapy or cytotoxic chemotherapy. [2]

title: Aspergilloma -- Deterrence and Patient Education
content: The management of aspergilloma and CCPA has evolved over the past decade with the introduction of new antifungal agents and alternative nonsurgical and surgical therapies. However, due to the relatively low prevalence of the disease, many clinicians have limited experience, particularly in developed regions. This limited experience is further compounded by reliance on somewhat outdated studies in the medical literature. Controversy exists regarding certain therapeutic and follow-up strategies, as well as a lack of clinical studies. These challenges complicate the diagnosis and management of this complex infectious disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE and antifungals for systemic use represent two distinct therapeutic categories within the broader class of antifungal agents. Antifungals for systemic use are employed when fungal infections require absorption and distribution throughout the body, whereas dermatological antifungals are applied topically to treat skin‑limited infections. The systemic antifungal category includes specific agents such as terbinafin, which is a member of the systemic antifungal group. Although both categories share the common goal of eradicating fungal pathogens, they differ in route of administration, site of action, and clinical indications. Recognizing the distinction between dermatological and systemic antifungal therapies is essential for selecting the appropriate treatment strategy and optimizing patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE represent a therapeutic class that includes both ANTIFUNGALS FOR TOPICAL USE and ANTIFUNGALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE. By definition, topical antifungal agents are applied directly to the skin, nails, or scalp to treat superficial fungal infections, whereas systemic antifungal agents are administered orally or intravenously to reach deeper or more extensive dermatologic sites. The inclusion of both topical and systemic formulations under the umbrella of ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE reflects the clinical reality that dermatologic fungal conditions can be managed with either local therapy for mild or localized disease or systemic therapy for more severe, widespread, or refractory infections. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate route of administration based on infection severity, pathogen susceptibility, and patient factors, ensuring comprehensive care for dermatologic fungal disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
content: Itraconazole, fluconazole, and voriconazole are members of the class Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics. As triazole‑based antifungal agents, they share a common mechanism of inhibiting the fungal cytochrome P450 enzyme lanosterol 14α‑demethylase, thereby disrupting ergosterol synthesis and compromising fungal cell membrane integrity. Because of this shared pharmacologic action, these drugs are employed for the treatment of a broad spectrum of systemic fungal infections, including candidiasis, aspergillosis, and other invasive mycoses.
